main_ingredients: sliced white fish (e.g. batang / Spanish mackerel or snapper)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of sliced fish porridge is approximately 350 g (one bowl), providing around 196 kcal, 10.7 g protein, and 26 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Sliced Fish Porridge — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05100130 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice congee / rice porridge
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 78 is based on white rice congee / rice porridge values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2021). Sliced fish porridge is a congee-format dish made from well-cooked white rice; the fish and vegetable additions do not materially alter the GI of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 26.0) / 100 = 20.3 g for a 350 g bowl containing 26.0 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 272 mg per 100 g; a 350 g bowl delivers approximately 952 mg sodium, representing 40–48% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt and fish sauce in the stock, avoid adding soy sauce or preserved vegetables at the table, and limit portion size to one bowl to keep single-meal sodium below 1000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI of 78 due to gelatinisation of white rice starch; glycemic load per 350 g bowl is approximately 20.3 g, which is in the high GL range and will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, increase the proportion of fish and vegetables relative to rice, and pair the dish with a source of soluble fibre or vinegar-based condiment to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is low at 1.53 g per 100 g with saturated fat estimated at approximately 0.3 g per 100 g; fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with cardioprotective properties; the dish is not fried.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sliced fish porridge is generally suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat content and lean fish protein; sodium should be monitored and the dish should be prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from sliced fish have anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is very low; the dish is not deep-fried.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sliced fish porridge is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; request reduced salt in the stock and avoid nightshade-based condiments such as chilli sauce that may trigger inflammatory flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sliced Fish Porridge (鱼片粥, Yú piàn zhōu) is a classic Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of smooth, well-cooked white rice porridge (congee) topped with thin slices of fresh white fish — commonly batang (Spanish mackerel), snapper, or grouper — along with shredded ginger, spring onions, and leafy vegetables. The fish slices are typically poached directly in the hot porridge stock, yielding a silky, tender texture and a clean, delicate flavour. The broth is seasoned with light soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. Per 100 g, the dish provides 56 kcal, 3.06 g protein, 1.53 g fat, and 7.41 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 272 mg. The high water content of the porridge base results in a low caloric density, making it a popular choice for breakfast, recovery meals, and elderly or post-illness feeding. Clinically, the dish has a high glycemic index (GI 78) due to the gelatinisation of white rice starch, so portion control is important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 272 mg per 100 g is borderline for hypertensive individuals at typical serving sizes of 350 g. The lean fish content provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective benefits. The dish is halal-friendly, dairy-free, and low in saturated fat.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of fish porridge?
Is fish porridge good for diabetics?
Can I eat fish congee if I have high blood pressure?
What fish is used in Singapore fish porridge?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F05100130, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice congee analogous entry, which is the closest defensible approved GI source for this dish format. Saturated fat estimated at 0.3 g per 100 g based on the lean white fish and minimal oil composition consistent with HPB data.
notes: sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source — white rice porridge with fish and vegetables contains no added sugar and negligible intrinsic sugar. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient profile, and moisture data for this specific Singapore hawker preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F05100130 as of 2026; the HPB Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central do not publish these granular values for this composite hawker dish. Input dietary_tags incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based'; these have been corrected to false as the dish contains fish. GI source is an analogous estimate based on white rice congee from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singapore sliced fish porridge was found in the approved GI literature.
